displayName.text: Kyoto Wagyu Tonkatsu Ten no Meshi LA

text.text: Combining A5 wagyu with the deep fry tonkatsu style was pretty interesting. The Takatsu sauce completely masks the beef flavor, so you’d have to use something a lot more mild. I found myself wanting to taste the Wagyu beef without the fry. The sea scallop and roe was a nice starter. The matcha tea latte was delicious. Service was friendly. I’ll be back to try the tonkatsu and I’ll go get my wagyu NOT FRIED next time.  :)

originalText.text: Combining A5 wagyu with the deep fry tonkatsu style was pretty interesting. The Takatsu sauce completely masks the beef flavor, so you’d have to use something a lot more mild. I found myself wanting to taste the Wagyu beef without the fry. The sea scallop and roe was a nice starter. The matcha tea latte was delicious. Service was friendly. I’ll be back to try the tonkatsu and I’ll go get my wagyu NOT FRIED next time.  :)

text.text: Went here on a Monday night after workband it was the perfect time to go. Party of 4 walk in, arrived at like 5:30 pm because I'm at UCLA. The menu is not over whelming with options because you're going her for the katsu. I got the pork loin set but didn't spring for the extra ikura. Someone else my party did and that's the photo I have. It was excellent and clean tasting with the scallop appetizer which I failed to take a picture of. The service was impeccable, they checked in on our party and refilled our water. They even offered to refill the cabbage which I wish I had room in my tummy for. The katsu was perfectly crispy. The miso soup is smokey and satisfying. There's nothing more I can say except that it's yummy and reasonably priced for a set meal. Anyhow if you enjoy a good tonkatsu and you're on Sawtelle this is it. I noticed it got super busy when we were on the way out so be prepared for that. Also they have a fancy Japanese toilet in the ladies room, loves it. Parking in the back of the restaurant is free if you can snag a spot.

text.text:
Saw this spot on IG and decided to check it out for a Friday after-work dinner in Sawtelle.

There’s definitely a wait — but honestly, it’s worth it. Even with the place being busy, the servers were incredibly friendly and still took the time to chat and explain the menu. That kind of service always makes the experience better.

I ordered the A5 Wagyu set meal plus an à la carte pork tonkatsu. The Wagyu was unbelievably tender — rich, buttery, and literally melts in your mouth. The pork tonkatsu was just as impressive: crispy on the outside, juicy and flavorful on the inside, and not dry at all.

Great food, great service, and overall a solid tonkatsu spot. Definitely one of my newest favorite restaurants in Sawtelle.

originalText.text:

text.text:
Preface to say I am absolute tonkatsu-nerd in Tokyo. So my criticism might just be for a high bar I seem to set.

I had the rosu, hire combo set.

The not so good..

The pork  - it felt like the pork got served not as warm as I expected it to be. You can see the cutlets sitting on the holder for a bit too long, maybe due to opening rush.

I also felt the pork , the rosu was not as tender as it should be.

The service was a bit chaotic. I asked for a few things and it had to arrive in trickles  and I had to remind the staff a few times.

Now, the goods..

That scallop was excellent! That whole show around serving it added to the fun experience.

I liked the fact that they serve ayce miso soup, rice and cabbage like in Japan.

$35 for a full set was very reasonable!

text.text:
Today was the day, when LA is showered with heavy rain, helping a bit to 'tame down' the crowd and long line to this place. We had been passing by multiple times and seeing the extreme line making us cancel our plan to try the place out. Good news was they now offer virtual line via Yelp. Not sure what's wrong but the system's bit funky because we were first at the 3rd position on the line, then moving down to 5th, but nothing much to complain as it's not that far-off. We still waited for good 30 minutes before finally being seated.

The venue actually had decent space, but obviously couldn't handle the crowd hence the wait. Best indeed to come in smaller group as their table size wasn't huge and they did have some counter sitting. I always had love-hate relationship with restaurants that had live-cooking or open-kitchen concept as the smell did linger when dining in (frowned).

For this first go, we tried their US Wagyu Loin Set Meal, which was obviously one of their top 2 along with the A5. The set meal came with Soft-boiled Egg, Scallop Cutlet, Cabbage, Miso Soup, and Rice, with the last 3 offered for free-flow! It's a fun dining experience as they offered multiple ways to enjoy your meat; including salt, katsu sauce (original/spicy), garlic soy sauce, dashi soy sauce and wasabi, demi-glace sauce, or even dipping it into your soft-boiled egg! The wagyu was marrowy and sapid, but I had to admit I still preferred those I had in Tokyo. Needless to say, as this was available locally, certainly it's awesome alternative to cure the craving without having to fly half-day!

Trying out different kind of protein, we also opted for their Pork Tenderloin Culet, which I preferred compared to the Loin cut as I fancied leaner meat. It was decent, and probably for you who wanted to eat something different than beef. From the sauce, my top 2 would be their demi-glace sauce or simply the meat with wasabi.

Overall verdict, I would say worth coming back but I wouldn't wait beyond 30 minutes for it.
